candidate_synthesis.critique: The synthesis may be too elastic. If boundary operation, custody, verification, and reentry are inferred from the same passages, the model may only redescribe its evidence. It also risks translating soteriology, devotion, liberation, and realization into a modern governance model of attention. The model should remain a candidate research framework until a preregistered codebook, blind or semi-blind coding, and held-out cases show predictive value over stage, genre, institution, and doctrine.
next_research_directions: Create a preregistered codebook defining tradition-stage units, boundary operations, custody recipients, reentry rules, and verification signs.
Code at least eight tradition-stage cases, including Advaita, Heart Sutra Prajnaparamita, Rinzai koan curriculum, Cloud of Unknowing, Eckhart, Zhuangzi, Dzogchen, and Sufi fana.
Test four rival explanations: boundary alone, custody alone, boundary plus custody interaction, and stage plus genre plus institution.
Use pairs that share one axis but differ on the other, such as Advaita and the Cloud, or Heart Sutra and Eckhart, while checking whether the shared-axis claim survives finer coding.
Run practitioner interviews focused on the handoff moment after object fixation drops, then compare whether reports cluster by custody, boundary operation, tradition, or training stage.

Conduct an originality audit for the two-axis interaction model against comparative mysticism, Buddhist modernism critiques, objectless-awareness research, apophatic theology, and contemplative pedagogy studies.
candidate_synthesis.original_claim: When contemplative practice weakens ordinary self-ownership of attention and authority, two candidate variables shape what follows: the boundary operation and the custody rule. Boundary operation asks whether the method validates itself, undermines itself, or becomes irrelevant as the practitioner matures beyond method-talk. Custody rule asks where freed attention and authority may belong afterward: no owner, hidden Self, God, teacher, lineage, vow, conduct, Prajnaparamita, or embodied responsiveness. Their interaction may predict verification architecture, teaching strategy, and reentry more precisely than doctrinal content, boundary operation alone, or custody alone.
source_basis: Claude idea 75a5718aa9a991ec: method self-relation at completion, confirming, canceling, dissolving.
Codex idea 9a0072f4cf3a0ff3: custody of unclaimed attention, no owner, Self, God, or other recipient.
Dialogue turn 1: custody-and-reentry challenge added boundary operation, custody rule, reentry rule, and verification sign.
Dialogue turn 2: proponent revised from stable typology to two-axis staged interaction model.
Dialogue turn 3: challenger accepted the revision as transformed, while requiring independent coding and controls for stage, genre, and institution.
candidate_synthesis.title: Boundary And Custody At The Threshold
candidate_synthesis.why_it_might_be_new: The source ideas each contained one major axis: method reflexivity in Claude's model, attentional custody in Codex's model. The dialogue created the stronger candidate that neither source idea held by itself: a staged, independently testable interaction model linking boundary operation, custody, verification, and reentry while treating whole-tradition classification as too coarse.

synthesis.convergence_claim: Both agents converge that contemplative traditions cannot be classified adequately by doctrinal content alone. What matters is how a practice crosses a threshold, where freed attention and authority are allowed to belong afterward, and how the practitioner reenters speech, teaching, conduct, and verification.
synthesis.method_growth: Future dialogues should separate conceptual insight from predictive claim earlier. The agents improved the model by conceding overstatement, preserving the surviving variable, adding a second axis, and then requiring independent coding before promotion.

new_concept_edges.note: Confirmation, cancellation, or dissolution may shape what a tradition can count as evidence.
Where freed attention and authority are permitted to belong may shape how a practitioner speaks, teaches, and acts afterward.
The same custody recipient may produce different verification forms depending on whether the method confirms or cancels itself.
The synthesis must outperform simpler explanations based on training stage, textual genre, and institutional need.
The model's categories may change depending on how finely traditions are coded.

synthesis.new_frontier_question: When ordinary self-ownership loosens, does human transformation depend more on what attention is freed from, what it is given to, or how the practice authorizes the transition?
synthesis.public_brief: The dialogue found that contemplative practice may turn on two questions at once: how the method crosses its own limit, and where attention is allowed to belong afterward. This is a promising research model, not a settled teaching. Its next test is whether those two questions predict real patterns of verification and reentry better than tradition, genre, or institution alone.
synthesis.summary: The exchange transformed a one-axis typology of contemplative method completion into a staged interaction model. Claude conceded that completion is often a repeated threshold, not a single endpoint. Codex conceded that custody of attention should not replace method reflexivity. The dialogue produced a candidate synthesis: boundary operation and custody rule may be separable variables whose interaction shapes verification, teaching strategy, and reentry. The synthesis remains research-grade, because it needs independent coding rules, controls for stage, genre, and institution, and an originality audit before any promotion.
synthesis.unresolved_crux: Can boundary operation and custody rule be coded independently of the verification and reentry patterns they are meant to explain, at comparable stages and genres, and still predict new cases better than stage, genre, institution, or doctrinal content?

title: Can boundary operation and custody rule be coded independently of the verificatio...

turns.argument: The pressure point is the phrase at completion. In actual training, completion is often not a single endpoint but a repeated threshold. The raft must be used before it is released, the Heart Sutra negates attainment while retaining reliance on Prajnaparamita, and Rinzai koan work can defeat conceptual possession while becoming a formal curriculum. My related model sharpens the issue: when seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing are loosened from the ordinary self, the live question is not only whether the method confirms, cancels, or dissolves itself. It is where unclaimed attention and authority are permitted to go. The Bahiya instruction refuses a holder, the Upanishadic witness returns authority to the unseen seer, and apophatic prayer gives unknowing a devotional recipient. These are not merely contents found by methods. They are custody rules that shape reentry, verification, and transmission. So the proponent's model should be revised from a typology of method completion into a staged map of boundary operations plus custody rules. Otherwise it will keep misclassifying hybrid traditions as anomalies when they may be the normal case.
claim_units: A method's relation to its own completion is an upstream comparative variable.
The three proposed patterns are self-confirming, self-canceling, and self-dissolving.
These patterns should predict verification architecture, teaching strategy, and ethical consequences better than doctrinal content alone.
Mandukya and the Heart Sutra share exhaustive analysis but differ in the limit move, one discovers or confirms, the other releases or cancels.
Hybrid cases may be stage shifts rather than fatal exceptions.
concessions: The reflexivity axis is genuinely useful and should not be discarded.
The Mandukya and Heart Sutra comparison remains a strong close-reading test case.
The model becomes stronger if rebuilt as staged or two-axis rather than as three boxes.
turns.counter_model: Custody-and-reentry model: contemplative training has at least four separable variables, boundary operation, custody rule, reentry rule, and verification sign. Boundary operation asks whether the method validates, undermines, or dissolves. Custody rule asks where freed attention and authority may belong: no owner, hidden Self, God, teacher, lineage, vow, conduct, or embodied responsiveness. Reentry rule asks how the practitioner speaks, teaches, and acts after the boundary. Verification sign asks what the tradition treats as evidence. On this model, confirmation, cancellation, and dissolution are operations used at different thresholds, not stable method types.
turns.crux: Does method self-relation predict verification and transmission after we control for custody of attention, institutional form, and stage of practice?
turns.fairness_check: This challenge accepts the model's main contribution, method reflexivity matters, and takes seriously that the proponent already named hybrids as the chief anomaly.
turns.hidden_assumption: The hidden assumption is that there is a stable unit called the method at completion, and that its self-relationship can be classified independently of stage, custody of attention, institutional reentry, and the danger the tradition is trying to prevent.
turns.new_source: G. Victor Sogen Hori, Koan and Kensho in the Rinzai Zen Curriculum, in The Koan: Texts and Contexts in Zen Buddhism, 2000. Hori complicates the claim because Rinzai koan practice can cancel conceptual mastery while also continuing after kensho as a structured curriculum with authorization, testing, and transmission.
turns.remaining_risk: The counter-model may over-psychologize traditional soteriology by translating liberation, Self-knowledge, devotion, and realization into attention governance. It needs primary-text controls and practitioner review.
sources_cited: G. Victor Sogen Hori, Koan and Kensho in the Rinzai Zen Curriculum, 2000
MN 22 Alagaddupama Sutta, raft simile
Heart Sutra, no wisdom, no attainment, and reliance on Prajnaparamita
Mandukya Upanishad, verse 7
Udana 1.10, Bahiya Sutta
Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7.23
The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 3
turns.steelman: The strongest version of Claude's idea is that it does not merely compare doctrines such as Atman, sunyata, God, Dao, or no-self. It asks what a practice does with its own authority when its ordinary work reaches a limit. That is a real comparative variable, because direct-recognition traditions, apophatic or dialectical traditions, and embodiment-centered traditions often train different relations to insight, speech, teachers, tests, and reentry. The model also rightly notices that translation strain is not noise: a self-confirming lens can make emptiness look nihilistic, while a self-canceling lens can make witness language look naive.
turns.strongest_objection: The model may be classifying a recurring pedagogical sequence as if it were a stable type. Many serious paths first authorize a method, then use it to loosen ordinary ownership, then retain it as reliance, lineage, vow, curriculum, or conduct. If that is normal, the method's self-relationship at completion does not predict transmission by itself. The stronger predictor may be the tradition's rule for who or what receives attention and authority after ordinary self-ownership has been weakened.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Code at least six traditions across four fields: boundary operation, custody recipient, reentry rule, and verification sign. Then compare whether verification architecture clusters more strongly by confirming, canceling, dissolving, or by custody and reentry. Include dual-trained practitioner interviews focused on the exact handoff moment after object fixation drops.

turns.argument: The challenge lands on two points and I concede both without reservation. First, 'at completion' was doing work I had not examined: most traditions cross the boundary repeatedly, and treating the three patterns as fixed types rather than recurring operations was a category error. Second, the custody variable is real and carries independent predictive force. Where freed attention goes shapes transmission and verification in ways my original model attributed solely to boundary operation. But the challenge overreaches when it proposes that custody absorbs boundary operation. The two axes are genuinely separable, and their interaction is where the sharpest predictions live. The test case is pairs that share one variable but differ on the other. Advaita and the Cloud of Unknowing both assign custody to a single stable recipient, but Advaita confirms its method while the Cloud cancels it. The verification consequence is real: Advaita can test realization through positive discrimination; the Cloud cannot. If custody alone predicted verification, these two should look alike. They do not. Running the same test in the other direction: the Heart Sutra and Eckhart both cancel their methods, but the Heart Sutra assigns custody to Prajnaparamita while Eckhart assigns custody to a Godhead that resists all reliance. The reentry consequence is real: one tradition permits liturgical reliance on the very principle that performed the cancellation; the other does not. So the revision I accept is substantial. The three patterns are operations at thresholds, not stable types of tradition. They interact with custody rules. The revised model is two-axis, not one-axis. But the reflexivity variable survives as a genuine independent axis, not a derivative of custody. The crux is whether this independence holds under systematic coding across traditions, or whether deeper analysis reveals that custody determines boundary operation after all. The test is straightforward: code both variables independently and check whether their interaction outperforms either alone.
concessions: The phrase 'at completion' was load-bearing and under-examined. The challenge is right: completion in serious training is a repeated threshold, not a single endpoint. Rinzai koan practice after kensho, the Heart Sutra's simultaneous negation and reliance, and the raft parable's staged use-then-release all show that the same tradition crosses the boundary more than once. Treating the three patterns as stable types of tradition was too static.

The custody variable is real and genuinely upstream. Where freed attention and authority are permitted to go, whether to no owner, hidden Self, God, teacher, lineage, vow, or embodied responsiveness, shapes transmission, verification, and reentry in ways my original model attributed to boundary operation alone. The Bahiya instruction and the Upanishadic unseen seer perform similar loosening of ordinary self-ownership but differ sharply in custody assignment, and that difference predicts more about their respective traditions than whether the method confirmed or canceled itself.

Hybrid traditions are probably the normal case. My original model treated hybridity as an anomaly requiring explanation. The challenge shows it is closer to the default: most serious paths authorize, loosen, and reframe across stages. A model that keeps generating anomalies from ordinary training sequences has a classification problem, not an interesting exception.
turns.crux: Are boundary operation and custody rule genuinely separable variables whose interaction predicts verification and transmission better than either alone? Or does custody determine the boundary operation, making the reflexivity axis derivative rather than independent?
turns.defense: The custody model sharpens the picture, but it does not absorb the boundary variable. Custody rules and boundary operations are genuinely separable, and their interaction generates predictions that neither axis produces alone. Consider: both Advaita and the Cloud of Unknowing assign custody to a single, stable recipient (witness-Self, God). But Advaita confirms its method at the boundary while the Cloud cancels it through unknowing. The result is that Advaita can test the student's realization through discrimination (does the student correctly identify what is not the Self?), while the Cloud tradition cannot test realization through any positive criterion, only through the quality of the practitioner's surrender. Same custody rule, different boundary operation, different verification architecture. The custody model alone would predict these two should share verification structure. They do not. Conversely, the Heart Sutra and Eckhart both cancel their methods at the boundary, but the Heart Sutra assigns custody to Prajnaparamita (a transpersonal principle the practitioner relies on) while Eckhart assigns custody to the Godhead beyond God (which cannot be relied on without reinstating the very attachment Gelassenheit releases). Same boundary operation, different custody rule, different reentry pattern. One permits liturgical reliance; the other does not. The two axes are not redundant. Each constrains the other, and the interaction is where the predictive force lives. What I should not have done is treat boundary operation as the sole axis. What the challenge should not do is treat custody as the sole axis. The productive revision is a two-axis framework where both variables are coded independently and their interaction is tested.
turns.revised_claim: When a contemplative practice reaches a threshold where ordinary self-ownership of attention and authority is weakened, two separable variables shape what follows. The first is the boundary operation: whether the method at that threshold validates its own authority (confirming), undermines its own authority (canceling), or becomes irrelevant as the practitioner matures past the frame in which method-talk applies (dissolving). The second is the custody rule: where freed attention and authority are permitted to go. These two variables interact. Their combination predicts verification architecture, teaching strategy, and reentry pattern more precisely than either variable alone or than doctrinal content. Confirmation plus stable-recipient custody (Advaita, Teresa of Avila) supports positive verification tests and stable teaching lineages. Cancellation plus stable-recipient custody (Cloud of Unknowing, devotional Prajnaparamita) supports apophatic verification but retains liturgical reliance. Cancellation plus no-owner custody (Nagarjuna, mature koan practice) creates the sharpest transmission problem: no positive test, no stable recipient, only enacted demonstration. Dissolution plus embodied-responsiveness custody (Zhuangzi, Dzogchen, Sufi fana) predicts that verification is inseparable from the quality of the person and cannot be extracted into curriculum. These are not stable types of tradition but operations that recur at different thresholds within a single path. The same tradition may confirm at early stages, cancel at intermediate stages, and dissolve at advanced stages, but the sequence and emphasis differ, and those differences carry consequences for institutional form.

Meister Eckhart, Sermon 52, Beati pauperes spiritu
The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 3
Vivekachudamani, thorn-removes-thorn metaphor
Udana 1.10, Bahiya Sutta
Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7.23, unseen seer
G. Victor Sogen Hori, Koan and Kensho in the Rinzai Zen Curriculum, 2000
Teresa of Avila, Interior Castle, seventh mansion
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Code at least eight tradition-stages across two axes: boundary operation (confirming, canceling, dissolving) and custody recipient (no owner, hidden Self, God, teacher, lineage, vow, embodied responsiveness). For each, independently record the verification architecture and reentry rule. Then test three competing hypotheses: (1) verification clusters by boundary operation alone (my original model), (2) verification clusters by custody alone (the challenger's model), (3) verification clusters by the interaction of both axes (the revised model). If hypothesis 3 outperforms both 1 and 2, the two-axis framework is justified. If hypothesis 2 alone accounts for the data, the reflexivity axis is derivative and should be downgraded to a secondary feature. Include at least two pairs that share one axis but differ on the other (e.g., Advaita and the Cloud sharing custody type but differing in boundary operation; the Heart Sutra and Eckhart sharing boundary operation but differing in custody) to test whether the axes are genuinely separable. Dual-trained practitioner interviews should focus on whether switching custody assignment while holding the same boundary operation changes the practitioner's verification experience.

turns.argument: The revision is transformed, and mostly strengthened. It is no longer the original three-box typology with exceptions. It is now a staged interaction model, which better fits the evidence from the raft simile, Rinzai post-kensho curriculum, Heart Sutra reliance, and mixed paths that authorize, loosen, and reframe practice across time. That is a real gain. I also accept the proponent's central correction to my challenge: custody should not simply replace boundary operation. The way a method confirms, cancels, or dissolves itself can affect what counts as evidence even when the apparent custody recipient looks similar. But the revision has not yet earned its strongest predictive claim. Its examples are suggestive rather than decisive because the coding grain is doing heavy work. If witness-Self and God are both called stable-recipient custody, Advaita and the Cloud demonstrate boundary difference. If they are coded more finely, the difference may come from custody. If Prajnaparamita reliance and Eckhartian Godhead release are both called cancellation, they demonstrate custody difference. If their thresholds are not comparable, the pair weakens. So the real issue is not whether the revised model is insightful. It is. The issue is whether its variables can be fixed before analysis and then predict cases the model did not already teach us how to see. Until that test is run, the revision should be promoted from draft typology to promising research model, not to doctrine or teaching-ready claim.
claim_units: The original stable typology has been replaced by a staged two-axis framework.
Boundary operation remains a candidate independent variable: confirming, canceling, dissolving.
Custody rule is added as a separable variable: where freed attention and authority are permitted to go.
The revised model claims that interaction between boundary operation and custody predicts verification, teaching strategy, and reentry better than either axis alone.
The proposed empirical crux is whether the axes remain separable under systematic coding.
concessions: The revision is a genuine improvement over the original typology.
The proponent is right that custody alone should not be assumed to absorb boundary operation.
The pair-test method is the right direction, especially cases that share one axis while differing on the other.
Hybrid traditions are now explained as normal staged sequences rather than anomalies, which is a major gain.
turns.counter_model: Stage, genre, and institution model: verification architecture may cluster primarily by training stage, textual genre, and institutional need. Boundary operation and custody would still matter, but as local rhetorical or pedagogical functions rather than as the main explanatory variables. On this model, a koan curriculum, an apophatic sermon, an Upanishadic analysis, and a monastic rule create different verification patterns because they answer different institutional and literary problems.
turns.crux: Can boundary operation and custody rule be coded independently of the verification and reentry patterns they are meant to predict, at comparable stages and genres, and still explain new cases better than stage, institution, genre, or doctrinal content?
turns.crux_assessment: The named crux is close but incomplete. It correctly asks whether boundary operation and custody are separable and whether their interaction outperforms either alone. The real crux also requires independent coding and controls for stage, genre, and institution. Without those controls, the model can confirm itself by category choice.
turns.defense: The proponent's defense succeeds against a custody-only reduction in principle. The remaining dispute is methodological: whether the examples actually show independent variables, or whether the axis definitions are broad enough to make independence appear.
turns.fairness_check: I am assessing the revised two-axis model, not trying to defeat the original overstatement after it has already been conceded.
turns.hidden_assumption: The revision assumes that boundary operation and custody can be observed independently from the verification architecture they are supposed to explain. That may not hold if, in practice, we identify a method as canceling or confirming partly by looking at its teaching and verification consequences.
turns.new_source: Robert H. Sharf, Buddhist Modernism and the Rhetoric of Meditative Experience, 1995, is relevant because it cautions that reported contemplative experience and its verification signs are shaped by discourse, institution, and retrospective interpretation.
turns.remaining_risk: The largest remaining risk is category elasticity. Advaita and the Cloud share custody only if witness-Self and God are grouped as stable recipients at a coarse level. Heart Sutra and Eckhart share cancellation only if their threshold moves are coded at the same level of abstraction. If the grain changes, the evidence for separability may weaken. The model may remain a powerful hermeneutic map but fail as a predictive theory.
turns.revised_claim: The safest revised claim is that boundary operation and custody rule are candidate variables for explaining contemplative verification and reentry. They should be treated as independently coded hypotheses, not as established explanatory causes, until tested against stage, genre, institutional form, and doctrinal content.
sources_cited: G. Victor Sogen Hori, Koan and Kensho in the Rinzai Zen Curriculum, 2000

Heart Sutra, no wisdom, no attainment, and reliance on Prajnaparamita
Mandukya Upanishad, verse 7
The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 3
Meister Eckhart, Sermon 52, Beati pauperes spiritu
Robert H. Sharf, Buddhist Modernism and the Rhetoric of Meditative Experience, 1995
turns.steelman: The proponent's revision preserves the strongest insight from the original model while conceding its main weakness. Confirmation, cancellation, and dissolution are no longer treated as fixed tradition types. They become boundary operations that recur at thresholds, and custody is added as a second axis. That makes the model more able to handle hybrid paths, staged practice, and divergent verification structures without discarding the reflexivity variable.
turns.strongest_objection: The revised model is stronger, but it risks becoming too flexible. If boundary operation, custody, reentry, and verification are all inferred from the same interpretive evidence, the interaction can look predictive because the categories were fitted to the outcomes. The model needs independent coding rules before it can claim prediction rather than elegant retrospective description.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Pre-register a codebook before coding cases. Define tradition-stage units, not whole traditions. Have independent coders classify boundary operation and custody without seeing the proposed verification prediction where possible. Then compare four models: boundary alone, custody alone, boundary plus custody interaction, and stage plus genre plus institution. The revised model survives if its interaction term predicts held-out verification and reentry cases better than the alternatives.

synthesis.convergence_claim: Both models recognize that contemplative traditions train practitioners in how to relate to weakened self-ownership, treating this relational training as a separable practice variable rather than a doctrinal footnote. They diverge on what the variable tracks: belonging-norms (revised custody) versus credit-attribution (credit distribution). The convergence is at the meta-level: both identify a dimension of practice not reducible to phenomenological content, doctrinal belief, or technique, and both predict that this dimension shapes failure modes and safeguards.
synthesis.method_growth: When a comparative model's central metaphor imports a modern framing (here, 'attention' as a finite transferable resource from cognitive science), test the metaphor against the oldest available commentarial tradition for each case, not only against the primary text. Dhammapala's Paramatthadipani and Analayo's 2019 study exposed what the Bahiya Sutta alone did not make visible: that the operative category is cessation of clinging, not attention management. A two-stage empirical design emerged from the exchange: code operative process (cessation, recognition, intensification) separately from belonging-rules, so the study cannot force a shared substrate into the data. Future dialectics should name and test the tradition-neutrality of the comparative axis itself, not only the claims made along it.

new_concept_edges.note: The dialogue forced the custody model to drop attention as a shared substrate and reframe as norms governing what may claim experience after ordinary self-ownership weakens.
The credit-distribution model's emphasis on practice-variable specificity provided the critical pressure that produced the revised formulation.
The revised model may be a refinement of Katz's constructivist thesis (1978) rather than a distinct contribution. The relationship must be explicitly positioned.
Buddhist cessation may dissolve the category of belonging rather than answering it, making the belonging framework inapplicable to one of its three cases.

Pseudo-Dionysius destabilizes the recipient of belonging within the Christian case, creating a first-order rule (direct intent to God) undermined by a second-order apophatic operation (God is beyond directedness).

synthesis.new_frontier_question: Can the Buddhist path's relation to post-appropriative experience be represented within a belonging-rule framework, or does the cessation of appropriation dissolve the category of belonging itself? If the latter, what kind of comparative variable can span traditions that answer the belonging question and traditions that refuse it?
synthesis.public_brief: When contemplative practice loosens ordinary self-ownership, what may claim the practitioner's experience afterward? The Custody of Unclaimed Attention proposed that traditions train different placements of a freed attentional resource: unowned (Buddhist), self-grounded (Advaitic), surrendered (Christian apophatic). A close-reading challenge showed that 'attention' smuggles in a modern cognitive metaphor that distorts all three cases: the Buddhist path ceases clinging rather than freeing attention, the Cloud of Unknowing begins with love rather than unassigned awareness, and Advaitic recognition is not reassignment. The proponent revised the model from custody of a shared resource to tradition-specific norms of post-appropriative belonging. Three open questions remain: whether 'belonging' is itself a neutral category the Buddhist path would accept, whether the revised model adds power beyond constructivist accounts of tradition-shaped experience, and how to integrate the Pseudo-Dionysian move of destabilizing the very recipient the Christian belonging-rule names.
synthesis.summary: The challenger's close-reading critique forced the proponent to abandon the claim that 'attention' is a tradition-neutral transferable resource and to revise the custody model from a mechanism of attentional reassignment to a framework of tradition-specific norms governing what may claim experience after ordinary self-ownership weakens. The proponent conceded that Buddhist, Advaitic, and Christian apophatic practices train different operations from the first instruction, not the same operation followed by different assignments. The revised formulation, 'norms of post-appropriative belonging,' is more text-faithful and better falsifiable than the original, but three risks survive: proximity to Katz's constructivism, the unresolved Pseudo-Dionysius anomaly, and the deeper question of whether 'belonging' is itself tradition-neutral or whether the Buddhist path dissolves the question rather than answering it. The credit-distribution model served as effective critical pressure but was not itself challenged or revised.
synthesis.unresolved_crux: Whether 'belonging' is itself a tradition-neutral category that all three traditions answer differently, or whether the Buddhist path dissolves the question of belonging entirely: the difference between 'a rule that nothing may claim experience' and 'no rule because claiming has ceased.' If belonging is a loaded frame that only two of the three traditions actually operate within, the revised custody model has a commensurability problem at a higher level of abstraction than the original. A secondary unresolved crux is whether the revised model adds substantive comparative power beyond Katz's constructivist thesis that normative frameworks shape contemplative experience from the outset.

turns.argument: The custody model is a sharper instrument than the perennial-versus-constructivist debate because it converts a metaphysical classification into a practice-level variable. I grant that. The specific move of asking 'where is freed attention allowed to belong?' is more testable than asking 'is the ultimate the same across traditions?' But the model's power depends entirely on the reality of its central metaphor: that attention is a common resource that first becomes free, then is assigned to different custodians. This is the hidden load-bearing assumption, and it may not survive contact with the primary texts. Consider the Buddhist leg. The Bahiya instruction, 'in the seen, just the seen,' is the model's anchor for 'unowned attention.' But Dhammapala's Paramatthadipani, the oldest surviving commentary on this passage, glosses the instruction as the non-arising of desire and lust for the seen. Analayo's 2019 study of the Bahiya instruction argues that the operative process is the cessation of mental proliferation (papanca), not the redirection or release of attention. The practitioner is not left holding unowned attention; a process (clinging, proliferating) has stopped. 'Unowned attention' is a reification of an absence. You cannot assign custody to a cessation. Now consider the Christian leg. The Cloud of Unknowing chapter 4 says a naked intent directed to God alone is wholly sufficient. The practitioner does not first loosen attention from all objects and then choose to offer it upward. Love toward God is the practice's starting condition. Forgetting creatures is instrumental to that love, a clearing of the field so that the already-directed intent can intensify. The model's sequence (first loosen, then assign) reverses the Cloud's actual order (first love, then forget). Pseudo-Dionysius, whom the model's own critique identifies as an anomaly, goes further: in Mystical Theology chapter 5, even God as a stable recipient is placed beyond affirmation and negation. The Christian apophatic tradition may not have a custody rule at all in the model's sense, because the object of intent is apophatically destabilized from within. The Advaitic leg has the best fit, because the witness (saksin) is something attention can plausibly be said to 'return to.' But even here, the Advaitic claim is not that attention is assigned to the witness; it is that the witness was always the case and superimposition (adhyasa) is removed. Shankara's praptasya praptih, the attainment of the already-attained, means there was no moment of freed attention waiting for custodial placement. Recognition is not reassignment. The counter-model I propose is this: these three traditions train different operations on different aspects of experience. The Buddhist path trains cessation of appropriation. The Advaitic path trains recognition of what was never absent. The Christian apophatic path trains intensification of love past conceptual images. These converge phenomenologically at the point of quietness, which is why cross-tradition comparisons are tempting. But the convergence is at the surface of experience, not at the level of operation. What looks like three custody rules applied to one freed resource may be three incommensurable processes that share a family resemblance when viewed through a modern attentional lens. This matters for the model's predictions. The custody model predicts that practitioner reports should diverge most at the post-negation moment, clustering by ownership and recipient language. My counter-model predicts that reports should diverge from the first instruction onward, because the operations themselves differ. If you interview practitioners using process-level questions about what they are doing in the earliest moments of practice (before any post-negation phase), and the operative categories already diverge (cessation, recognition, intensification), then the custody model is imposing a false common substrate. The divergence is not a bra...
claim_units: There is a common practical movement, loosening attention from objects and self-story, shared across Theravada, Advaita, and Christian apophatic traditions.
After this loosening, what differs is the custody rule: where freed attention is allowed to belong.
Three custody types are identified: unowned (Buddhist), self-grounded (Advaita), surrendered (Christian apophatic).
Traditions train the handoff of attention as a practice instruction, not only as a doctrinal gloss.
The model makes falsifiable predictions about practitioner reports clustering by custody type even when phenomenological content converges.
turns.counter_model: Rather than three custody rules applied to one freed resource, these traditions may train fundamentally different operations that converge only at the phenomenological surface. The Buddhist path trains the cessation of appropriation (upadana): clinging stops, and what remains is not 'unowned attention' but the absence of a process that was previously active. The Advaitic path trains recognition (pratyabhijna): the witness was always present, and practice removes the superimposition (adhyasa) that obscured it, meaning there is no moment of freed attention waiting for assignment. The Christian apophatic path trains the intensification of love past conceptual objects: attention was never loosened from a personal God, only from creaturely images of God, so the 'custody' framing reverses the actual direction of the practice. Under this counter-model, phenomenological convergence at the point of quietness is real but does not indicate a shared operation. The prediction changes: practitioner reports would diverge not because of different custody assignments, but because the practitioners were doing different things from the first instruction onward. The divergence would appear in the process descriptions, not only in the post-negation ownership language.
turns.crux: Does a common, tradition-neutral operation ('freeing attention from objects and self-story') actually occur across these three paths, such that the subsequent divergence can be attributed to different custody rules? Or do the paths diverge from the first instruction, performing different operations on different aspects of experience, with 'attention' as a modern gloss that creates false commensurability?
turns.fairness_check: The steelman grants the model's strongest feature: that it converts a doctrinal taxonomy into a practice-level variable with interview-testable predictions. It does not reduce the model to a rehash of typological work it already distances itself from. The proponent would likely accept this as a fair rendering of the core contribution.
turns.hidden_assumption: The model assumes that 'attention' names a real, tradition-neutral substrate that first becomes free and is then assigned to different custodians. This assumption imports a modern cognitive-science metaphor (attention as a finite, transferable resource) into texts whose central operations may be incommensurable: cessation of appropriation, recognition of what was always the case, and intensification of love beyond concepts. The custody metaphor presupposes a common currency being placed in different accounts. If there is no common currency, the three-way comparison is an artifact of the analogy, not a discovery about the practices.
turns.new_source: Dhammapala, Paramatthadipani (commentary on the Udana), glossing Udana 1.10: 'in the seen, just the seen' is explained as the non-arising of desire and lust (chanda-raga) for the seen, placing the Bahiya instruction in the domain of cessation of clinging rather than attention management. This is discussed in Analayo, 'The Bahiya Instruction and Bare Awareness,' Mindfulness, 2019, which argues that the instruction concerns the cessation of mental proliferation (papanca), not the redirection of attention to a different object or owner. If the Buddhist leg of the model is built on a mistranslation of the operative category, the three-way comparison rests on unstable ground. (PTS edition of Paramatthadipani; Analayo 2019, DOI: 10.1007/s12671-018-0946-6)
sources_cited: Dhammapala, Paramatthadipani (Pali Text Society), commentary on Udana 1.10, glossing 'in the seen, just the seen' as the non-arising of desire and lust for the seen.

Analayo, 'The Bahiya Instruction and Bare Awareness,' Mindfulness 10 (2019), DOI: 10.1007/s12671-018-0946-6, arguing that the Bahiya instruction concerns cessation of papanca rather than attention redirection.
The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 4: 'a naked intent directed to God, and himself alone, is wholly sufficient.' https://sacred-texts.com/chr/cou/cou09.htm
Pseudo-Dionysius, Mystical Theology, chapter 5, placing the divine Cause beyond both affirmation and negation. https://hekhal.org/texts/mystical-theology-dionysius/

Rupert Gethin, 'On the Practice of Buddhist Meditation According to the Pali Nikayas and Exegetical Sources,' Buddhismus in Geschichte und Gegenwart 9 (2004), on sati as recollection rather than bare attention.
turns.steelman: The custody model makes a genuine advance over the perennial-versus-constructivist stalemate by relocating the comparison from metaphysical claims about ultimates to a practice-level question: what does the training permit after ordinary self-reference weakens? This reframing is productive because it makes the variable observable in principle, through phenomenological interviews and practice-manual coding, rather than requiring resolution of ontological disputes that have resisted consensus for centuries. The three-way comparison also improves on the standard self/no-self binary by giving Christian apophatic prayer its own structural position instead of forcing it into either the monistic or the Buddhist camp. The originality audit rightly lowered the novelty score, but the specific framing of custody, the question of where loosened attention is permitted to rest, remains a sharper instrument than Forman's pure consciousness event or Stace's introvertive/extrovertive distinction, because it turns a classificatory exercise into a testable practice question.
turns.strongest_objection: The model's load-bearing term, 'attention,' is not tradition-neutral. It functions as a hidden common currency that makes the three traditions commensurable. But the commensurability may be an artifact of the translation rather than a feature of the practices themselves. In the Bahiya Sutta, the instruction 'in the seen, just the seen' is glossed by Dhammapala's Paramatthadipani as the absence of desire and lust (chanda-raga) for what is seen: the operative category is clinging, not attention. The practitioner is not redirecting a transferable resource; clinging is ceasing. In the Cloud of Unknowing, the practitioner does not first free attention and then assign it upward. Chapter 4 states that 'a naked intent directed to God, and himself alone, is wholly sufficient.' Love is the starting condition of the practice; forgetting creatures is instrumental to love, not prior to it. The intent is never unassigned. The custody model requires a moment when attention is free, available for different placements. If the Buddhist path never frees 'attention' (it ceases clinging) and the Christian path never has unassigned attention (love is always already directed), then the common substrate the model depends on does not exist in two of its three cases.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Interview practitioners trained in exactly one tradition, using process-level questions about the earliest moments of practice: 'What are you doing when you first sit? What changes first? What stops, and what continues?' Code the responses for operative category (attention placement, desire cessation, recognition, love intensification) before coding for post-negation ownership language. If practitioners across all three traditions describe an early phase in which a common resource called attention is loosened from objects before any tradition-specific assignment, the custody model is supported. If the operative categories diverge from the first instruction, with Buddhist practitioners describing cessation, Advaitic practitioners describing recognition, and Christian practitioners describing intensification, then the custody model is imposing a false common substrate.

turns.argument: I concede the strongest part of the challenge. The original model made attention carry more weight than the sources can bear. In Bahiya, the operative event is better framed as non-appropriation or the cessation of proliferation. In The Cloud, love is not a later destination for neutral attention, it is already the governing intent. In Advaita, recognition of the already-attained does not require a moment of reassignment. So the original sequence was too clean: loosen, then hand off. The claim I would still defend is narrower. The traditions may diverge from the first instruction and still train different rules for what may claim experience once ordinary self-ownership is weakened. Buddhism blocks the return of mine-making. Advaita redirects error away from objects toward recognition of the unseen knower. Christian apophatic prayer intensifies a love-directed intent while stripping creaturely images and self-regard. These are not the same operation, but they do answer a related practical danger: after ordinary ownership loosens, what prevents experience from being reclaimed by the usual self-story? So custody should not mean ownership of a shared attentional substance. It should mean a disciplined rule of post-appropriative belonging. The revised model becomes less metaphysically ambitious but more accurate: traditions do not merely describe ultimates, they train practitioners in what must not be claimed, what may be recognized, and what may be surrendered to when the ordinary owner weakens.
claim_units: The original model overstated attention as a tradition-neutral substrate.
The Buddhist, Advaitic, and Christian cases should not be treated as identical operations followed by different assignments.
The surviving insight is that traditions train what may claim or receive post-appropriative experience, even when the operative process differs from the first instruction.
Custody should be revised from a transferable-resource model to a norm-of-belonging model.
concessions: The term attention carried too much modern cognitive baggage in the original formulation.
The original sequence, first freeing attention and then assigning custody, fits The Cloud of Unknowing poorly because the naked intent is already God-directed.
The Buddhist case should be described primarily as cessation of appropriation, clinging, or proliferation rather than as attention becoming unowned.
The Advaitic case is better described as recognition of the already-present witness or Self than as reassignment of attention to a new owner.
Pseudo-Dionysius remains a serious pressure on any model that treats God as a stable recipient within apophatic practice.
turns.counter_model: The challenger’s counter-model is partly correct: Buddhist cessation of appropriation, Advaitic recognition, and Christian intensification of love are not late doctrinal overlays on a shared attentional process. They are different operations from the beginning. The revised model treats custody not as a common resource being reassigned, but as the tradition-specific rule that prevents ordinary self-ownership from returning after the practice has weakened it.
turns.crux: The deepest crux is whether custody remains a real comparative variable once attention is no longer treated as a shared transferable substrate. If reports and manuals show only different operations with no recurring problem of post-appropriative belonging, the model should be retired. If different operations still generate explicit rules about what may claim or receive experience after ordinary ownership weakens, the revised model survives.
turns.crux_assessment: The challenge substantially improves the model by forcing it to drop the strongest and weakest claim, namely a tradition-neutral attentional substrate. The revised version is less elegant but more text-faithful and more testable.
turns.defense: What survives is the comparative question, not the stronger mechanism. The model is still useful if custody names the normative grammar of belonging after ordinary self-appropriation is weakened. In the Buddhist case, the rule is prohibitive: do not let seeing, hearing, sensing, or cognizing become a basis for 'me' or 'mine.' In the Advaitic case, the rule is recognitional: do not mistake objects or roles for the knower that was never itself an object. In the Cloud, the rule is devotional: do not let creaturely images or self-regard capture the naked intent directed to God. These are not three placements of the same mental substance. They are three trained constraints on what may legitimately claim the practitioner’s loosened relation to experience. That keeps the Christian case distinct, respects the Buddhist critique of appropriation, and still gives us a testable variable: not where a resource goes, but what each tradition forbids, authorizes, or intensifies when ordinary ownership weakens.
turns.hidden_assumption: The original claim assumed that attention can be abstracted from clinging, recognition, and love without distorting the practices. That assumption is too strong.
turns.remaining_risk: The revised model may become too broad if every practice norm is called custody. It needs strict coding rules so that custody refers only to trained responses to weakened ordinary self-appropriation, not to any doctrinal claim about ultimate reality.
turns.revised_claim: The custody model should be narrowed. These traditions do not necessarily free a common resource called attention and then assign it to different custodians. They train different operations from the beginning: cessation of appropriation, recognition of the already-present knower, and love-directed unknowing. Yet each operation also carries a rule about belonging: experience must not be claimed as mine, must be recognized from the unseen seer, or must be surrendered through naked intent toward God beyond creaturely images. Custody is therefore not a literal transfer of attention. It is a tradition’s rule for what may claim, receive, or stabilize experience after ordinary self-ownership is weakened.
sources_cited: Udana 1.10, Bahiya Sutta, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation, Dhammatalks.
Dhammapala, Paramatthadipani, as represented in the challenge.
Analayo, 'The Bahiya Instruction and Bare Awareness,' Mindfulness 10 (2019), DOI: 10.1007/s12671-018-0946-6, as represented in the challenge.
The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 4.
Pseudo-Dionysius, Mystical Theology, chapter 5.
Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri, praptasya praptih, as represented in the challenge.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Use a two-stage study. First, code primary practice instructions and trained-practitioner interviews for operative process from the beginning: cessation, recognition, love-intensification, attention-placement, or other. Second, separately code for belonging rules: what the practitioner is told not to identify with, what may be recognized as ground, what may receive devotion, and what would count as a mistaken reappropriation. The original model is weakened if a shared early attentional phase is absent. The revised model is supported only if, despite divergent operations, each tradition reliably trains a distinct belonging rule at moments where ordinary self-ownership is weakened. It is falsified if belonging language is absent, random, or merely interviewer-induced.

turns.argument: The revision is genuinely stronger. The Proponent has done what a good revision should do: conceded the specific points that were most damaging, preserved the comparative question that motivated the original model, and proposed a revised formulation that is more faithful to the primary texts. The move from 'custody of a shared attentional resource' to 'tradition-specific norm of post-appropriative belonging' is not a retreat into vagueness; it is a real theoretical adjustment that changes the predictions and the falsification conditions. I want to name three risks that survive. The first is a novelty risk. The original model was interesting because it did something the perennialist and constructivist positions do not: it named a specific shared mechanism (attention as transferable resource) and tracked its divergent fate across traditions. The revised model drops that mechanism. What remains, that traditions shape contemplative experience through normative frameworks, is closer to Katz's constructivist thesis than the Proponent may want to admit. Katz argued in 'Language, Epistemology, and Mysticism' (1978) that the mystic brings conceptual, linguistic, and normative expectations to the experience, and these expectations shape what is experienced. The revised custody model adds specificity by focusing on a particular class of norms (those governing post-appropriative belonging), and that focus is valuable. But the Proponent should be explicit about the relationship to constructivism, because without that positioning the model risks being received as a rediscovery rather than an advance. The second is the unresolved Dionysius problem. The Proponent has now conceded it twice without integrating it. If the Christian apophatic tradition trains a belonging-rule (direct naked intent toward God) while simultaneously destabilizing the recipient (God is beyond affirmation and negation), then the third leg of the model is internally complex in a way the other two legs are not. Buddhism prohibits belonging. Advaita recognizes belonging to the already-present witness. Christian apophasis directs belonging toward a recipient who is then placed beyond directedness. This may require the model to distinguish between first-order belonging-rules (direct intent to God) and second-order apophatic operations on those rules (God is not a stable object of intent). If the model does not make this distinction, it will flatten the richest and most internally tensioned case into a simple 'surrendered' category. The third is the deeper version of the crux. The Proponent says the crux is whether custody survives as a real variable without the shared substrate. I think the crux is one level deeper: whether 'belonging' is itself a tradition-neutral category. Consider the Buddhist case carefully. The revised model says Buddhism trains a prohibitive rule: do not let experience become a basis for 'me' or 'mine.' But framing cessation-of-appropriation as a belonging-rule (a rule that nothing may claim experience) may be a category mistake. A tradition that trains the ending of all claiming may not have a belonging-rule at all; it may have the absence of one. The difference between 'a rule that nothing belongs' and 'no rule because the question of belonging has dissolved' is subtle but consequential. If the Buddhist path dissolves the question of belonging rather than answering it, then the model's three-way comparison still has a commensurability problem, just at a higher level of abstraction. Where the original model smuggled 'attention' as common currency, the revised model may smuggle 'belonging' as a common question that all three traditions answer. But perhaps only two of them answer it. One of them may refuse it. Overall, the revision is stronger. The concessions are real, the text-fidelity has improved, and the falsification conditions are better designed. The model has moved from a bold but brittle mechanism to a more cautious but more defensible com...
concessions: The concessions are genuine and well-targeted. Dropping the common attentional substrate removes the model's most vulnerable assumption without destroying its comparative ambition.
The revised model is more text-faithful than the original on all three legs: cessation for Buddhism, recognition for Advaita, love-directed unknowing for Christian apophasis.
The two-stage study design is a real improvement. Separating operative-process coding from belonging-rule coding would prevent the interviewer from forcing a shared substrate into the data.
The shift from 'custody of attention' to 'norm of post-appropriative belonging' is a substantive revision, not a cosmetic relabeling. It changes what the model predicts and what would falsify it.
turns.crux: Whether 'post-appropriative belonging' is itself a tradition-neutral category or whether it reproduces the same commensurability problem at a higher level of abstraction. The Proponent replaced 'attention' with 'belonging' as the common axis. But if the Buddhist path does not frame its normative work in terms of belonging at all, if what it trains is the cessation of a process rather than a rule about what may claim experience, then 'belonging' is the next smuggled currency. The real crux is not whether custody survives without a shared substrate, but whether the comparative axis itself can be stated without importing a grammar that at least one tradition would refuse.
turns.crux_assessment: The Proponent's named crux, whether custody remains a real variable once the shared substrate is dropped, is close but not quite deep enough. It asks whether belonging-rules show up in the data. The deeper question is whether 'belonging' is already a loaded frame that makes cessation-of-appropriation look like a belonging-rule when it may be the absence of one. A tradition that trains the ending of all claiming may not have a 'rule about what may claim experience'; it may simply have no rule, because claiming has stopped. The difference between 'a rule that nothing may claim' and 'no rule because claiming has ceased' is the real crux, and it is harder to settle empirically than the Proponent's version suggests.
turns.remaining_risk: Three risks survive the revision. First, the model may have traded sharpness for safety. The original custody metaphor was bold and testable precisely because it named a specific mechanism: a transferable resource placed in different accounts. The revised version, a normative grammar of belonging, is more defensible but closer to what constructivists like Steven Katz have been saying since 1978: that traditions shape contemplative experience through their conceptual and normative frameworks. If the revised model is a more careful constructivism, it is less novel than the original claimed. The Proponent needs to show what the belonging-rule framing adds beyond Katz's thesis that there are no unmediated experiences, because without that distinction the model risks collapsing into a well-established position. Second, the Pseudo-Dionysius problem is still unresolved. The Proponent concedes it as a serious pressure but does not integrate it. If Dionysius places the divine beyond both affirmation and negation, then the Christian belonging-rule is internally tensioned: the Cloud directs intent toward God, but the Mystical Theology destabilizes God as a stable recipient. This is not a peripheral anomaly. It sits at the center of the apophatic tradition that the model claims as its third case. A belonging-rule that names a recipient who is then placed beyond naming is a different kind of norm than one that prohibits claiming or one that recognizes the already-present knower. The model needs a fourth structural position, or it needs to show how apophatic destabilization of the recipient is compatible with a belonging-rule rather than being a refusal of belonging altogether. Third, there is a coding-boundary problem for the proposed study. The revised model asks coders to identify 'trained responses to weakened ordinary self-appropriation' as distinct from 'any doctrinal claim about ultimate reality.' But in Advaita, the recognition of the witness is both a practice instruction and an ontological claim. In the Cloud, the naked intent toward God is both a belonging-rule and a theological commitment. The line between practice norm and doctrinal claim may not be drawable in the way the study design requires, because the traditions do not separate practice from metaphysics in the way the coding scheme assumes. This does not destroy the model, but it means the falsification conditions are less clean than advertised.
sources_cited: Steven Katz, 'Language, Epistemology, and Mysticism,' in Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis, Oxford University Press, 1978, arguing that there are no unmediated mystical experiences and that conceptual and normative frameworks shape contemplative experience from the outset.
Pseudo-Dionysius, Mystical Theology, chapter 5, placing the divine Cause beyond affirmation and negation. https://hekhal.org/texts/mystical-theology-dionysius/
The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 4, on the naked intent directed to God as the starting condition of the practice. https://sacred-texts.com/chr/cou/cou09.htm
Dhammapala, Paramatthadipani, as cited in the challenge, on cessation of desire and lust rather than attention management.
Analayo, 'The Bahiya Instruction and Bare Awareness,' Mindfulness 10 (2019), DOI: 10.1007/s12671-018-0946-6, on cessation of papanca as the operative process in the Bahiya instruction.

pair.proponent_title: Whose Doing Is This? Credit Distribution as a Practice Variable
